data.title: Southern California Edison outlines $925M rebuilding plan after L.A. wildfires
data.description:
Edison International (NYSE:EIX) +1.9% in Monday's trading after saying late Friday it had submitted an initial plan to rebuild the Altadena and Malibu areas that were devastated by January's wildfires, including the replacement of more than 150 miles of damaged electrical power lines with underground lines.

Edison (NYSE:EIX) subsidiary Southern California Edison said it submitted the preliminary plan to California's Governor Newsom, and that the years-long project would cost in the $860M-$925M range while ultimately reducing the future risk of wildfire in the devastated communities.

The rebuilding plan includes undergrounding 40 circuit miles of distribution lines in Altadena and surrounding neighborhoods that have ben determined to be high fire risk areas and 23 circuit miles in non-high risk areas, along with 90 circuit miles in Malibu and the nearby high fire risk areas, and the company is evaluating the feasibility of an additional 19 circuit miles in Altadena.

Although no official cause for the fires has been released, several lawsuits have claimed that SoCal Edison power lines and towers in Altadena started the Eaton Fire, and Edison (EIX) has acknowledged the possibility of its equipment being involved in the cause of the Eaton Fire.

"SCE will build back a resilient, reliable grid for our customers," SoCal Edison CEO Steven Powell said. "We are increasing electrical capacity in fire-impacted communities and exploring advanced technologies, such as battery backup for critical care customers and smart panels."

data.title: Thomasville Bancshares GAAP EPS of $1.58
data.description:
• Thomasville Bancshares  press release (OTCPK:THVB): Q1 GAAP EPS of $1.58.
  • YTD Return on Average Assets of 2.27% and Return on Average Tangible Equity of 24.27%.
  • Total Assets of $1.899 billion, an increase of $156 million over the same period in 2024.
  • Loans grew to $1.592 billion, an increase of $158 million or 11% year-over-year.
  • Deposits grew to $1.642 billion, an increase of $134 million or 9% year-over-year.

data.title: Palantir's NATO deal places it in 'prime position' for AI in defense: Wedbush
data.description:
Today's revelation that NATO has acquired Palantir Technologies' (NASDAQ:PLTR) artificial intelligence-powered military system, known as Maven Smart System, has placed the Denver-based software company in a key position for more government wins in the future, according to Wedbush.

Palantir shares had climbed 6% by noon trading on Monday.

"While the terms of the deal were not disclosed and is set to be operational within the next 30 days, this was another win for Karp & Co. as more federal customers turn to PLTR for improved AI capabilities on the military front which is starting to heat up," said Wedbush analysts, led by Daniel Ives, in a Monday note. 

Wedbush reiterated its Outperform rating on the stock and its 12-month price target of $120. Palantir also remains a top name to own in 2025 as it continues to gain traction with commercial and federal contracts.

"We believe this deal represents an additional tailwind for PLTR with AI initiatives across both the US and European governments accelerating as AI remains a strategic focus on the federal front with Palantir in essence in the sweet spot to benefit from a tidal wave of federal spending on AI across North America and Europe," Ives added.

The Maven Smart System is already being utilized across all the U.S. military branches, including the Army, Air Force, Space Force, Navy and US Marine Corps. It secured a five-year contract valued at  $100M by the DEVCOM Army Research Laboratory last September.

data.title: Omeed Malik to join Fannie Mae's board, Pulte says
data.description:
Omeed Malik, a former Bank of America executive and founder of 1789 Capital, will join Fannnie Mae's (OTCQB:FNMA) board, effective immediately, Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte said via social media.

"Omeed brings great capital markets, legal, and Investment experience as we Make Fannie and Freddie Great Again!" Pulte wrote on X.

Malik is founder and CEO of Farvahar Partners, a boutique investment bank and broker/dealer, and 1789 Capital, an investment firm that provides financing to companies in the "Entrepreneurship, Innovation & Growth" economy, according to the Farvahar website.

1789 Capital invests in four areas: replication/parallel economy; deglobalization; ant-ESG (environmental, social and governance); and cutting edge technologies that disrupt industries weighed down by excessive bureaucracies, that firm's website said.

Before starting his own firms, Malik was a managing director and global head of the hedge fund advisory business at Bank of America Merrill Lynch.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Third Harmonic Bio plans to liquidate the company, sell urticaria treatment
data.description:
Third Harmonic Bio (NASDAQ:THRD) will be liquidating the company and selling its remaining assets, the company said on Monday. 

The California-based pharma company will be selling its assets including THB335, a treatment in chronic spontaneous urticaria.

The company estimates that the total distribution will be in the range between about $246.6M and $259.8M, or approximately $5.13 and $5.42 a share.

It also expects the initial distribution will be in the range of $246.6M and $255.4M, or $5.13 and $5.33 per share.

The company's board of directors has approved the plan to liquidate and will be seeking stockholder vote for the dissolution of the company at its annual meeting on June 5, 2025. If approved, the company will file a certificate of dissolution in the third quarter of 2025 to initiate delisting of its shares from Nasdaq. 

Stock of the company is up about 39% after the news.

data.title: Viking, Structure, Metsera on a tear as Pfizer drops obesity pill
data.description:
Shares of up-and-coming weight loss drug developers Viking Therapeutics (NASDAQ:VKTX), Metsera (NASDAQ:MTSR), and Structure Therapeutics (NASDAQ:GPCR) traded sharply higher after Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) announced plans to discontinue its once-daily oral obesity drug, danuglipron.

Other notable gainers included Terns Pharmaceuticals (TERN), Ventyx Biosciences (VTYX), and Altimmune (ALT), while major weight loss drug developers Eli Lilly (LLY), Novo Nordisk (NVO), Amgen (AMGN), and AstraZeneca (AZN) recorded modest gains.

In a statement before the market open, Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) said that a “potential drug-induced liver injury” seen in a patient who took part in a dose optimization study for danuglipron led to its decision, which will significantly cut its R&D prowess against obesity.

JPMorgan analyst Hardik Parikh views Pfizer’s (NYSE:PFE) setback as positive for other oral weight loss drug developers such as Viking (NASDAQ:VKTX) and Structure (NASDAQ:GPCR).

“Structure is now in line to become the 2nd small molecule oral to enter the market, and VKTX becomes an even more attractive partnership candidate,” Bloomberg News reported, quoting Parikh, who has overweight ratings on both stocks.

Piper Sandler analyst Biren Amin, who also rates Viking (NASDAQ:VKTX) with an Overweight recommendation, agreed, noting that “We view this as positive for VKTX given one less competitor.”

Meanwhile, William Blair analyst Andy Hsieh indicated VKTX's buyout prospects, noting that its oral weight loss candidate VK2735 will help PFE “reestablish not only a mere presence in obesity but also a leading position beyond the current Novo Nordisk-Eli Lilly duopoly.”

data.title: Short bets against materials stocks edge higher in March
data.description:
Short bets against materials' stocks rose in March compared to the end of February, with Amcor (AMCR) replacing Albemarle (ALB) as the most shorted materials stock.

Ecolab (ECL) was the least shorted in the pack.

Average short interest across S&P 500 materials stocks was 3.86% of shares float in end-March, up from 3.40% at the end of February. The S&P 500’s materials sector (XLB) fell 4.4% in the January-March period, compared to an over 8% drop in the broader market.

With the tariff narrative taking hold and the AI hype in the background for now, fears of an economic slowdown and higher inflation has created a shift from speculative investments to defensive options, one Seeking Alpha Author Konstantinos Kosmidis wrote, adding that "the fuel behind XLB's performance could be traced back to the chemicals' demand, which has been rising."

Stocks with the largest and least short positions

Ranked by short interest as a percentage of shares float

 

Amcor (AMCR) had 164.97M shares sold short as of March 31, or 11.49% of the shares float. Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings, the global packaging company has a Hold rating with a score of 3.17 out of 5.

With its approaching combination expected to deliver solid cash EPS accretion, Amcor would make a solid covered call candidate, The Insiders Forum said. The author expects AMCR to be range-bound until the consummation of the Berry transaction.

Albemarle (ALB) was the second most shorted stock at 11.08% of shares float, followed by International Paper (IP) and Celanese Corp A (CE) with short interest of 7.98% and 7.03%, respectively.

Turning to companies with the lowest short interest in the sector, Ecolab (ECL)  leads with a short interest of just 0.91%, followed by DuPont de Nemours (DD) with 1.16% short interest. 

Industry Analysis

 

 

Containers & packaging remains the most shorted industry within the sector, with 7.98% short interest as of the end of March, up from 6.66% at the end of February.

Chemicals stocks took the second spot with 2.57% short interest as of March 31, compared to 2.42% a month ago.

data.title: Consumers slightly reduce long-term inflation expectations, NY Fed says
data.description:
U.S. consumers have slightly tempered their inflation expectations for the long-term even as they hike their projections on short-term inflation, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York's March Survey of Consumer Expectations released on Monday.

The survey, which was conducted before President Donald Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs on April 2, showed that consumers are decidedly more pessimistic across many aspects of the U.S. economy, as well as their personal economies. Unemployment, job loss, and earnings expectations all deteriorated. 

Median inflation expectations increased by 0.5 percentage point to 3.6% at the one-year-ahead horizon, were unchanged at 3.0% for the three-year ahead timeframe, and declined by 0.1 pp to 2.9% for the five-year-ahead period.

Median year-ahead expected price growth rose by 0.1 pp for food to 5.2%, its highest level since May 2024, 0.7 pp to 7.9% for medical care costs, and 0.5 pp to 7.2% for rent. Median home price growth expectations, though, dipped by 0.3 pp to 3.0% in March.

In the area of labor markets, median year-ahead earnings growth expectations dropped by 0.2 pp to 2.8%, equaling its 12-month trailing average. Mean unemployment expectations — or the mean probability that the U.S. unemployment rate will be higher a year from now — jumped 4.6 pp to 44.0%, its highest level since April 2020.

Individuals also worried about losing their own jobs. The mean perceived probability of losing one's job in the next 12 months rose by 1.6 pp to 15.7%, the highest reading since March 2024.

In household finances, the median expected growth in household income fell by 0.3 pp to 2.8% in March, dipping below the 12-month trailing average of 3.0%. Meanwhile, median household spending growth expectations edged down by 0.1 pp to 4.9%. 

Perceptions of credit access fell, as well as expectations for future credit availability.

Not surprisingly, year-ahead perceptions about households' financial situations also worsened. The share of households expecting a worse financial situation one year from now climbed to 30.0%, its highest level since October 2023.

data.title: Meta begins training AI models with public content in EU
data.description:
Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) has expanded the training of its artificial intelligence models, and will utilize public content from users in the European Union, the social media company announced today.

To comply with EU regulations, private messages will not be used for training purposes, nor will any data from users under the age of 18. Beginning this week, EU users of Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp and Messenger, will receive notifications of the data collection. Meta said they have made it simple for users to object to data collection.  

"We believe we have a responsibility to build AI that's not just available to Europeans, but is actually built for them," Meta said. "That's why it's so important for our generative AI models to be trained on a variety of data so they can understand the incredible and diverse nuances and complexities that make up European communities. That means everything from dialects and colloquialisms, to hyper-local knowledge and the distinct ways different countries use humor and sarcasm on our products. This is particularly important as AI models become more advanced with multi-modal functionality, which spans text, voice, video, and imagery."

Meta has used a similar approach to train its AI models in other regions in the world.

"We're following the example set by others including Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) and OpenAI, both of which have already used data from European users to train their AI models," Meta said in a statement. "We're proud that our approach is more transparent than many of our industry counterparts."

Meta's flagship model is known as Llama. The company recently released some of its Llama 4 series models. Meta plans to hold LlamaCon, a new developer conference, on April 29.

data.title: State of trucking: Tariffs could extend the freight recession
data.description:
The shipments component of the Cass Freight Index declined 5.3% year-over-year in March to improve slightly from the 5.5% decline in February.

Cass Information System (CASS) highlighted that the recent 90-day pause on most reciprocal tariffs will likely lead to more pre-tariff shipping in Q2. However, the firm said that will contend with adverse effects from the extreme tariffs on China, and other tariffs.

"Volumes may also be temporarily supported in the coming months as consumers scoop up pre-tariff goods before prices go up. But thereafter, the trade war is likely to extend the for-hire freight recession as higher prices reduce goods affordability and consumers' real incomes," warned Cass.

After rising 13% in 2021 and 0.6% in 2022, the Cass Freight Index declined 5.5% in 2023 and 4.1% in 2024, and so far, is trending toward another decline in 2025.

Trucking, freight, and logistics stocks: Knight-Swift Transportation (KNX), Marten Transport (MRTN), Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), U-Haul Holding (UHAL), Landstar System (LSTR), Werner Enterprises (WERN), PACCAR (PCAR), Volvo Group (OTCPK:VOLAF), Daimler Truck (OTCPK:DTRUY), Cummins (CMI), Schneider National (SNDR), J.B. Hunt Transport Services (JBHT), XPO (XPO), Heartland Express (HTLD), TFI International (TFII), Universal Logistics Holdings (ULH), Covenant Logistics Group (CVLG), Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), Saia (SAIA), UPS (UPS), FedEx (FDX) and ArcBest (ARCB).
